class_schedule.topic: The Senior Thesis: Scope, Process, and Expectations
learning_objectives: Understand the scope and standards of a senior thesis in English literature
Develop a viable research question and preliminary bibliography
Establish a realistic timeline with milestones for a 30-40 page thesis
readings: Booth et al., The Craft of Research, Ch. 3-4 ('From Topics to Questions')
Sample senior theses from the department archive (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: The senior thesis is the culmination of your English literature education. It is an extended piece of original scholarship that demonstrates your ability to formulate a research question, conduct independent research, construct a sustained argument, and write at a professional level. This seminar supports you through the process.
key_concepts: The thesis as original scholarship: making a new argument, not summarizing existing ones
Research question development: from topic to question to working thesis
Scope management: a thesis that tries to do everything accomplishes nothing
The advisor relationship: how to work productively with your thesis advisor
Timeline and milestones: proposal, annotated bibliography, draft chapters, revision, defense
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop: each student presents their preliminary topic idea and receives feedback on scope, feasibility, and research question formulation from peers and the instructor

review.value: A topic ('gender in Victorian fiction') is not a thesis; a question ('How does Eliot's Middlemarch use scientific metaphor to challenge Victorian gender ideology?') is the beginning of one — the question must be specific enough to answer in 30-40 pages
The most common thesis failure is overreach. A focused argument about one text or a small group of texts is more valuable than a sweeping survey that covers everything superficially

class_schedule.topic: Research Methods and the Scholarly Conversation
learning_objectives: Navigate the scholarly conversation on your thesis topic using databases, bibliographies, and citation trails
Position your argument within existing scholarship: extending, challenging, or synthesizing previous work
Develop an annotated bibliography that maps the critical landscape
readings: Selected articles demonstrating how scholars position their arguments (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Your thesis does not exist in a vacuum. It enters a conversation among scholars who have already thought about your texts and questions. Understanding that conversation — and finding your place in it — is what makes your thesis scholarship rather than a very long essay.
key_concepts: The scholarly conversation: how to identify the key voices, debates, and positions on your topic
Citation trails: following references forward and backward to discover related scholarship
Positioning your argument: are you extending, complicating, or challenging existing work?
The annotated bibliography: not just a list of sources but a map of the critical landscape
Primary vs. secondary vs. theoretical sources and how each functions in your argument
lecture_notes.demonstration: Citation trail exercise: starting from one key article on your topic, trace its references backward (what it cites) and forward (what cites it) to build a network of related scholarship

review.value: Your thesis must demonstrate that you know what scholars have already said about your topic and that your argument adds something new — not necessarily a revolutionary insight, but a perspective, connection, or reading that the existing scholarship has not provided
An annotated bibliography does not just summarize sources — it evaluates them in relation to your project: how does each source relate to your argument, and what role will it play in your thesis?

learning_objectives: Present a formal thesis proposal including research question, working thesis, methodology, and chapter outline
Receive and integrate constructive criticism from peers and the instructor
Revise the proposal based on feedback before submitting to your thesis advisor

lecture_notes.introduction: The thesis proposal is your contract with yourself, your advisor, and the department. It commits you to a specific question, a specific methodology, and a realistic plan. Today you defend your proposal and improve it through feedback.
key_concepts: Proposal components: research question, working thesis, methodology, chapter outline, timeline, preliminary bibliography
The working thesis: a claim specific enough to guide research but flexible enough to evolve
Methodology: what theoretical or critical framework will organize your analysis?
Chapter outline: how will the argument develop across 30-40 pages?
Responding to feedback: distinguishing between feedback that improves your project and feedback that changes it into someone else's project
lecture_notes.demonstration: Formal proposal presentations: each student presents their proposal (10 minutes) followed by structured peer and instructor feedback (10 minutes) using a standardized response form

review.value: A thesis that is too rigid prevents discovery; a thesis that is too vague prevents focus. The working thesis should be specific enough to organize your research but open enough to accommodate what you learn along the way

The chapter outline should show how the argument develops: each chapter should advance the thesis by making a distinct claim, not merely treat a different text. The reader should be able to follow the argument's logic from the outline alone

class_schedule.topic: Draft Chapter Workshop: Giving and Receiving Scholarly Feedback
learning_objectives: Present a complete draft chapter for peer review
Provide detailed scholarly feedback on a peer's draft chapter
Integrate feedback into revision plans for the next draft
readings: Peer draft chapters distributed before class

lecture_notes.introduction: Today you workshop draft chapters — the real substance of your thesis. Peer review at this stage is scholarly feedback: you are reading as fellow researchers, not as writing tutors. Your job is to test the argument, challenge the evidence, and push the analysis deeper.
key_concepts: Scholarly peer review: evaluating argument, evidence, and analysis, not just prose style
Reading for argument: can you identify the chapter's claim and follow its logic?
Evidence evaluation: is the textual evidence sufficient, well-chosen, and well-analyzed?
The response letter: structured feedback that the writer can act on
Revision planning: turning feedback into a concrete revision plan with priorities and timeline
lecture_notes.demonstration: Structured peer review: each student reads one peer's chapter, writes a 500-word response letter, and presents key feedback in class discussion. The author listens, takes notes, and responds at the end.
setup_requirements: Draft chapters distributed one week in advance

review.value: At the thesis level, peer review is about the argument: is the claim clear? Is the evidence convincing? Are there counterarguments that need addressing? Is the analysis deep enough? Surface-level feedback (grammar, formatting) is secondary at this stage

A structured written response (500 words) that identifies the chapter's argument, evaluates its strengths, pinpoints specific areas needing development, and suggests concrete directions for revision — more useful than marginal comments alone

class_schedule.topic: Revision Strategies for the Full Thesis
learning_objectives: Apply systematic revision strategies to a full-length thesis
Ensure coherence and development across chapters
Prepare the thesis for formal submission and defense

lecture_notes.introduction: Revising a thesis is different from revising an essay. You must ensure that the argument holds across 30-40 pages, that chapters build on each other, and that the introduction promises what the conclusion delivers.
key_concepts: Global revision: reading the thesis as a whole for argument coherence and development
The introduction-conclusion contract: does the conclusion deliver what the introduction promises?
Transitions between chapters: how to connect distinct arguments into a continuous intellectual narrative
The reverse outline: summarizing each paragraph's role to expose structural problems
Final polish: formatting, Works Cited, proofreading, and preparation for defense
lecture_notes.demonstration: Revision workshop: students perform a reverse outline of their thesis, then identify structural problems, redundancies, and gaps — focusing on the argument's architecture rather than sentence-level issues

review.value: After writing the thesis, summarize the main point of each paragraph in one sentence. The resulting outline reveals the argument's actual structure, exposing paragraphs that repeat, digress, or fail to advance the thesis
The thesis defense is a conversation about your research: be prepared to explain your methodology, justify your interpretive choices, address limitations, and discuss directions for future research

learning_objectives: Defend the thesis in a formal academic presentation and Q&A
Articulate the thesis's contribution to the scholarly conversation
Reflect on the intellectual and personal growth achieved through the thesis process

lecture_notes.introduction: The thesis defense is both an examination and a celebration. You present your work to the seminar, defend your argument against questions, and reflect on what you have learned — not just about your topic but about yourself as a scholar and thinker.
key_concepts: Defense format: 15-minute presentation of the thesis argument, followed by 15 minutes of questions
Presentation skills: distilling a 30-40 page argument into a clear, compelling 15-minute talk
Responding to questions: treating challenges as opportunities to deepen the argument, not as attacks
Acknowledging limitations: intellectual honesty about what the thesis does not do
Reflection: how the thesis process has developed your skills as a reader, writer, and thinker
lecture_notes.demonstration: Formal thesis defenses: each student presents and defends their thesis before the seminar, with the instructor and a second faculty reader serving as examiners

review.value: A thesis defense is not an interrogation — it is a scholarly conversation about your research. The examiners' questions are invitations to demonstrate the depth of your knowledge and the sophistication of your thinking

Acknowledging what your thesis does not do — texts not addressed, theories not applied, questions left open — is not weakness but scholarly maturity. Every thesis has limitations; demonstrating awareness of them is a strength

major_assignments.title: Thesis Proposal
major_assignments.description: Submit a formal thesis proposal (2,500-3,000 words) including research question, working thesis, methodology, chapter outline, timeline, and annotated bibliography of at least 15 sources.
objectives: Formulate a viable research question for a senior thesis
Develop a working thesis and methodology
Map the scholarly conversation through an annotated bibliography
Create a realistic chapter outline and timeline
deliverables: Thesis proposal (2,500-3,000 words) with all required components
Annotated bibliography of at least 15 sources

criteria: Question is specific, arguable, and researchable
Question is appropriate in scope for a 30-40 page thesis
Working thesis makes a clear, defensible claim
Methodology is appropriate to the question and texts
At least 15 sources including primary texts, secondary criticism, and theoretical frameworks
Annotations evaluate each source's relevance and role in the project
Bibliography demonstrates thorough knowledge of the scholarly conversation
Sources are current, credible, and varied
Chapter outline shows how the argument develops logically
Timeline is realistic with appropriate milestones
Writing demonstrates command of scholarly prose
Proposal presentation is clear and responds to questions effectively
Viable, well-scoped research question with a clear working thesis
Thorough annotated bibliography demonstrating scholarly conversation knowledge
Logical chapter outline showing argument development
Professional proposal presentation and effective integration of feedback

major_assignments.title: Complete Senior Thesis
major_assignments.description: Submit a complete senior thesis of 30-40 pages (8,000-10,000 words) making an original argument about one or more literary texts, supported by scholarly research and close reading. The thesis must be defended in a formal 30-minute presentation and Q&A.
objectives: Produce an extended piece of original literary scholarship
Demonstrate mastery of close reading, theoretical analysis, and scholarly argumentation
Engage substantively with the scholarly conversation on your topic
Defend your thesis in a formal academic setting
deliverables: Complete thesis (30-40 pages, 8,000-10,000 words) in MLA format
Works Cited with all primary and secondary sources
1,000-word reflective essay on the thesis process and your development as a scholar

criteria: Thesis makes an original, arguable claim about the literary text(s)
Argument develops logically across chapters with each chapter advancing the thesis
Counterarguments and alternative readings are addressed
Conclusion extends the argument and identifies directions for future research
Thesis demonstrates thorough knowledge of the scholarly conversation
Secondary sources are engaged with substantively, not merely cited
Student positions their argument within and against existing scholarship
Theoretical framework is applied with sophistication
Textual evidence is abundant, well-chosen, and carefully analyzed
Analysis reveals new insights about the text(s)
Formal features are connected to thematic argument
Close reading demonstrates the highest level of analytical skill
Presentation distills the thesis argument clearly and compellingly
Student responds to questions with depth and confidence
Student demonstrates command of the broader scholarly context
Acknowledgment of limitations reflects intellectual maturity
Original, well-argued thesis demonstrating genuine scholarly contribution
Thorough engagement with the scholarly conversation
Exemplary close reading at the highest undergraduate level
Confident, articulate thesis defense

thesis_proposal.description: Formal thesis proposal with annotated bibliography

complete_thesis.description: Complete senior thesis (30-40 pages) with defense

process_portfolio.description: Drafts, revision history, peer feedback, and revision plans

seminar_participation.description: Quality of peer feedback, workshop engagement, and scholarly discussion

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all seminar meetings, especially proposal workshops and draft feedback sessions. Missing a workshop without prior arrangement results in a zero for that component.
course_policies.late_submissions: Thesis proposal and draft deadlines are firm. Late drafts delay the feedback cycle for the entire seminar. The final thesis deadline is absolute — no extensions.
course_policies.collaboration: All thesis work is individual original scholarship. Peer feedback in workshops is collaborative and expected. Consult your thesis advisor regularly.
course_policies.academic_integrity: The senior thesis must be entirely original scholarship. All sources must be properly cited. Plagiarism will result in failure of the course and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Word processing software with MLA formatting capability, university library database access, citation management software recommended, and LMS access.
